trial_name: PreVent 2 — Subglottic Suction & Polyurethane Cuff vs Standard ETT in Emergency Intubation (2025)
citation: Treggiari MM, Sharp ES, Ohnuma T, et al. Hospital and long-term outcomes for subglottic suction and polyurethane cuff versus standard endotracheal tubes in emergency intubation (PreVent 2): a randomised controlled phase 2 trial. Lancet Respir Med. 2025; published online Nov 27.
one_liner: In emergency intubation ICU patients, a polyurethane-cuff ETT with subglottic secretion drainage did not reduce ventilator-associated events or improve 6-month outcomes and was associated with a longer hospital stay.

pico.population: Adults (≥18 years) requiring emergency endotracheal intubation for acute respiratory distress/failure in the ED or hospital, with ICU admission and mechanical ventilation
pico.intervention: PU-EVAC ETT (polyurethane cuff with subglottic secretion drainage port); continuous suction at –20 mm Hg with manual air bolus (5–10 mL) every 4 hours
pico.comparison: Standard PVC-cuff ETT without SSD port
pico.outcome: Primary: laryngeal injury at 6 months. Secondary: VAC, IVAC, possible VAP, hospital/ICU length of stay, mortality, SF-36 scores, cognitive impairment at 6 months.

background.existing_knowledge: VAP and VAE are frequent complications of invasive mechanical ventilation. Microaspiration around the ETT cuff is a plausible mechanism. ETTs with SSD and polyurethane cuffs aim to reduce aspiration, but prior evidence focused on VAP incidence, not patient-centred outcomes.
background.knowledge_gap: Effects of SSD on mortality, long-term function, and airway injury in emergency intubation populations are uncertain. Older VAP-centric evidence may not generalize to current VAE/IVAC surveillance frameworks.
background.hypothesis: A polyurethane-cuff ETT with continuous SSD (PU-EVAC) would improve hospital and 6-month outcomes compared with a standard PVC-cuff ETT in adults undergoing emergency intubation.

methods.design: Randomised, controlled, phase 2 trial. Pragmatic enrolment under exception from informed consent (EFIC). Stratified randomisation by hospital and ETT size.
methods.setting: Two US academic hospitals (Yale New Haven Hospital; Oregon Health & Science University). Emergency department and in-hospital emergency intubations with subsequent ICU admission.

inclusion: Adults (≥18 years) requiring emergency endotracheal intubation for acute respiratory distress/failure in the ED or hospital
exclusion: Inability/unwillingness to participate at deferred consent stage

methods.intervention: PU-EVAC ETT (polyurethane cuff with subglottic secretion drainage port). Continuous subglottic suction applied at –20 mm Hg, with manual air bolus (5–10 mL) every 4 hours during intubation period.
methods.comparison: Standard PVC-cuff ETT without SSD port.
methods.blinding: Allocation concealment at enrolment via sealed, opaque envelopes and pre-prepared intubation kits. Clinical teams were not blinded post-intubation. Long-term outcome assessor blinding was not reported.
methods.sample_size_power: 1074 participants required to detect a 10% absolute difference in laryngeal injury incidence (assumed 40% in the PVC group) with 80% power at a two-sided 5% significance level. Planned group sequential design with one interim analysis.
secondary_outcomes: Hospital and ICU length of stay
SF-36 Physical and Mental Component Scores at 6 months
methods.follow_up: Index admission outcomes through ICU/hospital discharge. 6-month follow-up for laryngeal injury, quality of life, and cognition.

headline_bullets: No significant difference in the primary outcome of laryngeal injury at 6 months (83% vs 70%, P=0.098), but analysis was limited to only 157 participants.
No reduction in VAC, IVAC, or possible VAP with the intervention.
Hospital length of stay was significantly longer in the PU-EVAC group (26.0 vs 22.0 days, P=0.046).
Mortality was similar between groups at all time points.

outcomes_table.note: Assessed only in participants completing 6-month follow-up (n=157).

outcomes_table.outcome: SF-36 Physical Component Score at 6 months
outcomes_table.note: Assessed only in participants completing 6-month follow-up (n=157).

outcomes_table.outcome: SF-36 Mental Component Score at 6 months
outcomes_table.note: Assessed only in participants completing 6-month follow-up (n=157).
Assessed only in participants completing 6-month follow-up (n=157).
Only statistically significant between-group difference among major in-hospital endpoints.
Reported as counts/percentages; effect estimate not provided.

safety: Laryngeal injury was numerically higher with PU-EVAC (83% vs 70%) among the small subset completing 6-month evaluation.

domains.notes: Computer-generated randomisation with stratification by hospital and ETT size; allocation concealment via sealed, opaque envelopes and prepared intubation kits. EFIC enrolment enabled randomisation at the point of emergency intubation.
Open-label post-intubation design with potential for performance bias, although major infection outcomes were algorithmic (CDC/NHSN VAE).

Of 1074 enrolled, 1068 were included in ITT analyses. For 6-month outcomes, attrition was substantial: 558 deaths, 167 non-consents, and 186 lost to follow-up; only 157 completed follow-up, limiting power and introducing potential selection bias.
43 crossovers occurred; a per-protocol analysis showed similar findings. Reintubation was managed by re-randomisation, adding analytic complexity.
Groups were broadly comparable, but there was an imbalance in intubation location: ED intubation 64% (PU-EVAC) vs 54% (PVC).

internal_validity.verdict: Internal validity is moderate: allocation concealment and pragmatic enrolment support credibility for in-hospital endpoints, but the very low completion of 6-month assessments materially limits inference for long-term outcomes and airway safety signals.

domains.notes: Emergency intubation ICU patients represent a common real-world pathway. Exclusions (elective OR intubations, pre-existing tracheostomy, tube sizes outside 7.0–8.0) limit applicability to other populations.
Findings are most applicable to resource-adequate systems able to deploy continuous subglottic suction protocols and prevention bundles similar to those in US academic ICUs.

external_validity.verdict: External validity is moderate: the emergency intubation ICU population is clinically relevant, but the two-centre US academic setting and device/size constraints restrict broader generalisability.

strengths: Large pragmatic randomised trial (n=1068 analysed) in an emergency intubation pathway where randomisation is logistically challenging.
Use of standardised CDC/NHSN VAE framework (VAC/IVAC/possible VAP), aligning outcomes with contemporary surveillance constructs.
Prespecified 6-month patient-centred outcomes (laryngeal injury, quality of life, cognition) beyond traditional VAP endpoints.
Allocation concealment at point of intubation with stratification by site and ETT size.
limitations: Open-label post-intubation design with potential for performance bias.
Very low completion of 6-month follow-up (157/1068), limiting power and increasing risk of selection bias for long-term outcomes.
Reintubation with re-randomisation introduces analytic complexity and potential contamination across exposure groups.
Device 'dose' (–20 mm Hg suction with scheduled air bolus) may not be generalisable to other SSD systems or suction settings.

critical_appraisal.summary: A well-designed pragmatic trial with moderate internal and external validity, showing no benefit of a PU-EVAC tube on VAE or long-term outcomes, but the primary and key secondary long-term endpoints are severely underpowered due to high attrition.
strengths: Pragmatic, EFIC-based enrolment in a challenging emergency setting.
Use of objective, algorithmic VAE surveillance definitions.
weaknesses: Critically low follow-up rate for the primary outcome, rendering it uninterpretable.

sections.authors_conclusion: In emergency intubation ICU patients, PU-EVAC did not reduce VAC/IVAC/possible VAP and was associated with longer hospital length of stay, arguing against routine 'device-first' adoption in this context without clearer benefit.

clinical_bottom_line.verdict: This trial does not support the routine use of PU-EVAC tubes for emergency intubation in the ICU. The intervention showed no benefit on VAE or long-term outcomes and was associated with a longer hospital stay.
clinical_bottom_line.applies_to: Adults requiring emergency intubation in the ED or hospital who are admitted to the ICU, in settings with established VAP prevention bundles.
clinical_bottom_line.does_not_mean: This does not mean SSD tubes are harmful, but that any potential benefit is not large enough to be detected in a contemporary VAE framework, and the signal for increased length of stay warrants caution.
implementation_caveats: The primary outcome (laryngeal injury) and other 6-month outcomes are uninterpretable due to very high loss to follow-up.
The suction protocol (–20 mm Hg continuous) is specific and may not apply to other SSD systems.
The trial was conducted in two US academic centres, which may limit generalisability.

controversies.topic: Reassessment of SSD as a default prevention strategy
controversies.notes: The accompanying editorial argued that the null findings on VAE/VAP outcomes and lack of demonstrable long-term benefit should prompt reconsideration of SSD tubes as routine practice in emergency intubation pathways.

The 2022 SHEA/IDSA-aligned guidance framed SSD as an 'unresolved issue' (downgraded from earlier recommendations), reflecting heterogeneity and uncertainty in older VAP-focused evidence; PreVent 2 adds a large pragmatic emergency intubation trial to this evidentiary tension.

Updated syntheses have reported reductions in VAP incidence with SSD, but device heterogeneity, varying suction protocols, and outcome ascertainment differences complicate translation to patient-centred endpoints. PreVent 2 suggests that any VAP signal may not map cleanly to VAC/IVAC or long-term outcomes in contemporary care.

context_related_trials.relation: A prior trial by the same group evaluating a similar intervention, likely informing the design and hypothesis of PreVent 2.

Another large trial evaluating a different intervention (continuous vs intermittent beta-lactam infusion) in sepsis, mentioned in the context of shifting from surrogate to patient-centred outcomes in critical care trials.

unresolved_questions: A formal updated meta-analysis incorporating PreVent 2 (and stratifying by setting, suction 'dose', and outcome framework: VAP vs VAE/IVAC) is needed to reconcile older VAP-focused evidence with contemporary emergency-intubation data.

quick_recall.one_line_takeaway: PU-EVAC tubes do not reduce VAE or improve long-term outcomes after emergency intubation and may prolong hospital stay; routine use is not supported.
